name: Santa Maria della Concezione Crypts
description: The crypts of Capuchin friars decorated with the bones of over 4,000 friars, including an entire "crypt of pelvises."

name: Torre Argentina (Roman Cat Sanctuary)
description: Hundreds of lucky felines haunt the ruins where Caesar was murdered.

name: Galleria Sciarra
description: This hidden Art Nouveau courtyard is an opulent feast for the eyes.

name: Quartiere Coppedè
description: Architectureal fantasy quarter of Rome.

name: The Dome Illusion
description: A master of trompe-l'oeil creates a stunning, tourist-fooling dome out of a flat ceiling.

name: The Aventine Keyhole
description: Rome's semi-secret peephole vista is also a former Crusader stronghold.

name: The Mouth of Truth
description: The yawning maw of this pagan visage is said to bite off the hands of liars.

name: Porta Alchemica
description: An alchemist's "magic door" stands in the middle of a Roman park.

name: Pyramid of Cestius
description: The only "Egyptian" pyramid in Europe and the legendary tomb of Remus.

name: The Mithraeum at Circus Maximus
description: The underground sanctuary of a mysterious ancient cult.

name: St. Valentine's Skull
description: The skull of the patron saint of lovers lies in the Basilica di Santa Maria in Cosmedin—maybe.

name: Via Appia
description: 2300-year-old Roman road connecting the empire.

name: Santa Maria dell'Orazione e Morte
description: At the crypt of St. Mary of Eulogies and the Dead you are left alone to ponder mortality among piles of skulls.

name: Zuccari Palace
description: Architectural monsters are devouring this palace's every door and window.

name: The Protestant Cemetery
description: The final resting place of the poets Shelley and Keats.

name: Parco degli Acquedotti
description: This beautiful park on the outskirts of Rome protects the ruins of two colossal ancient aqueducts.

name: Pasticceria Boccione
description: In Rome’s Jewish ghetto, a 200-year-old bakery still makes the neighborhood’s historic sweets.

name: Domus Aurea
description: The engineering wonder and pleasure dome of a much-maligned emperor still holds undiscovered secrets.

name: Vicus Caprarius
description: A forgotten "city of water" underneath the famous Trevi district.

name: The Catacombs of San Sebastian
description: Jesus' footprints and the first catacombs in the world.

name: Casina delle Civette
description: A fairy tale castle in modern Rome.

name: Campo de Fiori
description: This ritzy Italian market is built around a statue of an unforgiven heretic who was burned for his belief in an endless universe.

name: Museo Storico Nazionale Dell' Arte Sanitaria
description: One of the most important yet well-hidden museums dedicated to the history of medicine.

name: Museum of the Holy Souls in Purgatory
description: A collection of objects supposedly singed by the hands of souls in purgatory.

name: Saint Victoria's Incorruptible Body
description: The martyr's wax-enhanced skeletal remains lie preserved in a glass case in the Santa Maria della Vittoria in Rome.

name: Passetto di Borgo
description: The Pope’s secret escape route.

name: Servian Wall at McDonald's
description: Inside this McDonald's you can dine on your Big Mac and fries next to a 2,500-year-old Roman wall.

name: The Head of St. John the Baptist at San Silvestro in Capite
description: One of several skulls claiming to belong to the beheaded St. John the Baptist is on display at this Roman church.

name: Papal Hearts at Santi Vincenzo e Anastasio a Trevi
description: This former Roman Catholic Church still contains the embalmed hearts and organs of 22 popes.

name: Mausoleum of Augustus
description: This large, circular tomb is the final resting place of the first emperor of Rome.

name: Basilica di San Clemente
description: A Nesting Doll of Churches.

name: Metropoliz Museum of the Other and the Elsewhere
description: An abandoned Roman salami factory has found new life as an art space home to 200 squatters.

name: Ospedale delle Bambole (Doll Hospital)
description: A hospital where antique dolls are given new life.

name: Mussolini's Balcony
description: The spot where the fascist dictator made some of his most famous speeches including declaring war on Britain and France.

name: Arch of Gallienus
description: This Roman arch marks the location of an ancient gate at one of the Seven Hills of Rome.

name: Trevi Waterfall
description: A magnificent waterfall is also home to the remains of a Roman hydraulic engineering system.

name: Meridian Line of the Basilica of St. Mary of the Angels and the Martyrs
description: Installed in an act of one-upsmanship, the annual sundial in this church is also a symbolic victory for the Gregorian calendar.

name: Villa Doria Pamphili Park
description: A huge enchanting public park just outside the ancient walls of Rome.

name: Mercatus Traiani (Trajan's Market)
description: This ancient market is often referred to as the oldest shopping mall in the world.

name: Vigna Randanini
description: One of seven Jewish catacombs in Rome and one of only two open to the public.

name: Tempietto at San Pietro in Montorio
description: More sculpture than building, this architecturally significant temple was built on the spot of Saint Peter's crucifixion.

name: Mamertine Prison
description: This ancient Roman prison is decorated with an upside-down cross not as blasphemy but as saintly tribute.

name: Temple of Hercules Victor
description: This curious, round temple is the oldest still-standing marble building in Rome.

name: Lacus Curtius
description: A mysterious chasm in the heart of the ancient Roman Forum, the Lacus Curtius was once believed to be a gateway to hell.

name: Keats-Shelley Memorial House
description: Museum dedicated to literary greats of the Romantic Period is also the home in which Keats met his untimely end.

name: Stumbling Stones of Rome
description: Brass cobblestones mark the places where individual Holocaust victims were taken from their homes.

name: Monte Testaccio
description: This hill made entirely of ancient Roman jars was simply a garbage dump to the people of the time.

name: Basilica of Santa Sabina
description: The best preserved Paleochristian basilica in Rome hides a number of oddities and secrets.

name: Parish Church of Santa Maria del Popolo
description: A treasure trove of Renaissance-era funerary art in a Roman church built on top of Nero's grave.

name: The Water Clock
description: This hydrochronometer was invented by a Dominican monk and is a prime feature in this garden.

name: Cloaca Maxima
description: The "Great Sewer" of ancient Rome is one of the oldest sewer systems in the world, and is still in use.

name: Pons Fabricius
description: This is the oldest Roman-made bridge in the city still existing in its original state.

name: Natural Monument of Galeria Antica
description: An Italian ghost town that fought obscurity, until a mysterious  illness brought it to its knees.

name: The Sacconi Rossi Crypt
description: A burial vault located under Tiber Island in the middle of the Italian capital.

name: The Wax-Encased Remains of Blessed Anna Maria Taigi
description: The mystic whose incorrupt corpse still attracts the faithful.

name: Ludus Magnus
description: The ruins of the largest gladiatorial school in Rome are largely hidden under modern streets.

name: Borromini's Perspective Gallery of Palazzo Spada
description: This gallery commissioned by Cardinal Spada is known for its magnificent optical illusion.

name: Vespa Museum
description: This subterranean shrine beneath a bike rental store celebrates the iconic Italian scooter.

name: Palazzo Naiadi Roman Baths
description: Ancient ruins hidden underneath a modern luxury hotel.

name: Cinecittà
description: The studio built by fascists in the hopes of "reviving the film industry" with the occasional propaganda film opportunity thrown in.

name: Tarpeian Rock
description: In the early Roman Empire, people deemed traitors and criminals were tossed to their deaths from this rock.

name: The "Little London"
description: A modern sliver of architecture in Roma.

name: Rome's Gladiator School
description: This historic recreationist school churns out warriors that may very well have had a fighting chance in Rome's pits of the day.

name: Elephant and Obelisk
description: A detailed pachyderm supports Rome's smallest Egyptian obelisk.

name: Veii
description: Rome's ancient Etruscan rival, now a park full of ruins.

name: The Museum of Roman Ships at Fiumicino
description: Roman-era ships unearthed...

name: Via Sacra
description: This ancient trail climbs the mountain where a Latin League cult worshipped Jupiter, its main deity.

name: Centrale Montemartini
description: Gods clash with machines in a power plant regenerated as a museum.

name: Temple of Portunus
description: This small, ancient Roman temple was once a church dedicated to the patron saint of penitents.

name: Cappella di San Zenone (Chapel of Saint Zeno)
description: Its interior is adorned with some of the best examples of early Christian mosaics.

name: Santa Francesca Romana
description: St. Francesca Romana, patron saint of  drivers, resides in the church she founded.

name: The Martyr Murals at Santo Stefano Rotondo
description: Charles Dickens was not a fan of the ghastly wall dressings in this 6th century church.

name: Saint Catherine Russian Orthodox Church
description: The first Russian Orthodox church in the Catholic city of Rome in almost a thousand years.

name: The Flying Donkey
description: Rome's first modern graffito is all that is left of a peaceful protest from the 1970s.

name: Pons Aemilius
description: The only remaining arch of the oldest stone bridge in Rome stands in the middle of the Tiber.

name: Palazzo della Civiltà Italiana
description: Also known as the Square Colosseum, this building is a prime example of fascist-era rationalist architecture.

name: Garum Biblioteca e Museo Della Cucina
description: Rome is now home to a stunning food museum and library.

name: Piè di Marmo (Marble Foot)
description: A massive marble foot is tucked away in a small alley near the Pantheon.

name: Casa Romuli
description: These hut foundations are believed to be the residence of Romulus, founder of Rome.

name: Basilica of Santi Quattro Coronati
description: A fortified medieval basilica on the Caelian Hill preserves extraordinary frescoes and one of Rome's most beautiful hidden cloisters.

name: Abandoned Abbey of Santa Maria del Piano
description: The ruins of an abbey believed to have been founded by Charlemagne in the 9th century.

name: Fontana dell'Acqua Felice
description: A monumental fountain in Rome features a large statue of Moses that has been criticized for centuries.

name: Tor Fiscale Park
description: This park on the outskirts of Rome boasts two ancient Roman aqueducts and a medieval tower.

name: Nymphaeum of Egeria
description: Roman politician Herodes Atticus built this nymphaeum to honor his wife, whom he may have murdered.

name: Basilica of San Crisogono
description: Remnants of a 4th-century church with vivid medieval frescoes and ancient sarcophagi hide beneath this old basilica.

name: 'Il Babuino' ('The Baboon')
description: Romans decided this 16th-century "talking statue" was so ugly, they named it after a primate.

name: Basilica of Saint Paul Outside the Walls
description: One of Catholicism's four major basilicas has a series of papal portraits that are said to predict the end of the world.

name: Sant'Ambrogio e Carlo al Corso
description: This basilica is dedicated to two saints, one of which is known more for his famous quote.

name: The Sweating Cenotaph at the Archbasilica San Giovanni in Laterano
description: Stone memorial that's said to predict the death of the pope and the site of the Cadaver Synod.

name: Arch of Janus
description: This one-of-a-kind Roman arch is not actually dedicated to the god Janus.

name: Passetto del Biscione
description: This hidden alley near the location of Rome's first theater was the site of a curious miracle.

name: Profondo Rosso
description: Italian horror legend Dario Argento’s own shop hides a sinister museum-dungeon in the basement.

name: Il Tempio di Adriano (Temple of Hadrian)
description: The remains of this 2nd-century Roman temple are incorporated into a modern building.

name: Bunker Soratte
description: Hidden within this iconic mountain are a set of World War II bunkers once used by German forces.

name: Porto di Ripetta
description: An 18th century fountain and two columns are all that remains of Rome's forgotten river harbor.

name: Tomba di Nerone (Tomb of Nero)
description: Known for centuries as the famous Roman emperor's burial place, this monument is actually something else.

name: Sedia del Diavolo
description: According to local folklore, this Roman sepulcher was once a throne for the Devil.

name: Stadio dei Marmi
description: This Italian stadium makes liberal use of fascist statuary to evoke classical sporting arenas.

name: Antoninus and Faustina Temple
description: An ancient Roman temple first dedicated to an emperor's wife is now a church.

name: Triangolo Barberini
description: An abandoned 17th-century baroque hunting lodge with a wonderfully strange architectural plan.

name: Sisinnius Fresco in the Basilica San Clemente
description: A rather vulgar example of the transition from Latin to the Italian vernacular.

name: The Globe of the Vatican Obelisk
description: Legend has it that this bronze orb, which sat atop an ancient Egyptian obelisk, once held the ashes of Julius Caesar.

name: Il Facchino
description: A statue representing a lost Roman profession that was vandalized for centuries.

name: Madama Lucrezia
description: This statue is the only female representation of Rome's "talking statues."

name: Ponte Nomentano
description: One of Rome's most picturesque and original bridges was also a popular choice for Grand Tour artists.

name: Arco dei Farnesi
description: This unassuming bridge is most likely one of Michelangelo's unfinished masterworks.

name: Secret Compartment in Leonardo da Vinci Statue
description: A secret hatch containing two parchments was discovered in this statue over 40 years after its installation.

name: Arch of the Argentarii
description: This ancient Roman arch misled treasure hunters for centuries.

name: Orto Botanico di Roma
description: An oasis of beauty, tranquility and botanical research in the middle of Rome.

name: Hendrik Christian Andersen House Museum
description: The former home of an Italian artist is now a shrine to his ambitious works.

name: Temple of Minerva Medica
description: Believed to be the remains of the lost Temple of Minerva, this nymphaeum has been known by its misnomer to this day.

name: Casa dei Crescenzi
description: A rare example of a medieval house in Rome adorned with a melancholic inscription.

name: Ponte Lupo
description: A colossal aqueduct from the second century B.C., deeply hidden in a gorge on the outskirts of Rome.

name: Palazzo Malta
description: Inside this building is another country within Rome, a sovereign entity without a physical territory.

name: Durandal's Mark
description: A deep mark on the remains of a Roman temple, believed to be left by a legendary warrior.

name: Wheel of the Exposed
description: An unremarkable wooden wheel built into the side of one of Rome's oldest hospitals was part of a policy to reduce infant exposure.

name: Sculpture of Giovanni Paolo II
description: A sculpture serving as an homage to Pope John Paul II.

name: Santa Maria sopra Minerva Flood Markers
description: Plaques on its facade commemorate the Tiber's most destructive deluges.

name: Chiesa di San Francesco a Ripa Grande (Church of San Francesco a Ripa)
description: A small cell adjacent to this church was once the bedroom for a renowned saint.

name: San Nicola in Carcere
description: This small church was constructed over the ruins of three temples dating to the Roman Republic.

name: Hermitage of Saint Sylvester
description: This hermitage celebrates the legend of Emperor Constantine's healing from leprosy.

name: Buca Dello Spione
description: An 18th-century post box used to alert the church of sick and ailing foreign pilgrims.

name: Gelateria dei Gracchi
description: Since 1999, this gelateria founded by a string instrument maker has been turning out some of Rome’s best scoops.

name: Anita Garibaldi Monument
description: The Italian revolutionary's legendary wife is portrayed on horseback waving a gun and holding her child in this memorial on the Janiculum.

name: Supplizio
description: Crispy, cheesy rice balls—the Roman answer to arancini—take center stage here.

name: Fontana dei Libri
description: A small but very fascinating fountain in the center of Rome with four books and a deer carved.

name: Er Buchetto
description: For more than a century, this hole-in-the-wall near Termini Station has been serving what some say is the greatest porchetta sandwich.

name: Wunderkammer Roma Artificialia
description: This room of wonder is full of relics, from knights in armor to rare works of art.

name: Arch of Dolabella
description: This small Roman arch was transformed into a dwelling by the founder of the Trinitarian Order.

name: Torquato Tasso's Oak Tree
description: The charred, dead skeleton of an oak tree is said to have consoled a dying 16th-century Italian poet.

name: Flavio al Velavevodetto
description: For one of Rome’s finest renditions of cacio e pepe, head straight to this osteria.

name: Tomb of Eurysaces the Baker
description: A freedman baker’s lavish tomb has a relief showing various stages of Roman bread making.

name: Arco di Malborghetto
description: The meaning behind this triumphal Roman arch was a mystery until the early 20th century.

name: Monument to the Fallen of Dogali
description: An ancient Egyptian obelisk is now used to commemorate fallen Italian soldiers.

name: Basilica of San Giorgio in Velabro
description: This medieval church in Rome was almost destroyed following a terrorist attack in 1993.

name: 'The Brave Righetto'
description: Among the busts of heroes of the Roman Republic of 1849, there is also a statue of a boy and his dog.

name: 'Res Gestae Divi Augusti' ('The Deeds of the Divine Augustus')
description: A large replica of a monumental Roman inscription is located on the side of this museum.

name: Museo Barracco di Scultura Antica
description: An expansive collection donated by an heirless nobleman formed this admission-free civic museum.

name: Fratelli Trecca
description: Roman-style pizza al taglio arrives here with magnificent, crackly crust.

name: Casa di Lorenzo Manilio
description: A 15th-century monumental Latin inscription on this house celebrates Lorenzo Manilio's love for Rome.

name: C'è Pasta... E Pasta!
description: Fried artichokes and other Roman–Jewish classics are executed with care at this no-frills cafeteria.

name: Cesare al Pellegrino
description: Chef Leonardo Vignoli subtly updates Roman classics in a storied space.

name: Piatto Romano
description: Once the home of a renowned slaughterhouse, formerly working-class Testaccio is still the place to go for Roman offal dishes.

name: Pier Paolo Pasolini Park
description: A literary park dedicated to the memory of a filmmaker, writer, and intellectual who was killed in 1975.

name: Giano Restaurant
description: Inventive Sicilian specialties are executed with flair at this restaurant overseen by chef Ciccio Sultano.

name: EUR Piezometric Water Tower
description: Nicknamed “The Mushroom,” this unique water tower has been featured in several iconic movie scenes.

name: Santa Costanza
description: This immaculately preserved church from late antiquity was originally the mausoleum of the Emperor Constantine's daughter.

name: Fontana della Terrina
description: When this 400-year-old fountain fell into disrepair, officials covered the mess up with what looks like a giant soup tureen.

name: L’Osteria della Trippa
description: Everything from fried brains to stewed lungs are on the menu at this nose-to-tail spot.

name: The Great Synagogue of Rome
description: A square-domed symbol of resilience in the heart of the old Jewish Ghetto.

name: Tower of Centocelle (Torre di Centocelle)
description: A medieval tower, dating back to the 12th century, lingers in the Roman countryside.

name: The Catacombs of Priscilla
description: See one of the earliest depictions of a Christian ceremony – with a woman at the center!

name: The Great Mosque
description: Striking fusion of Islamic and postmodern architecture, said to be the largest mosque in Western Europe.

name: Museo Nazionale delle Paste Alimentari
description: This delightful little museum is dedicated to only one thing: pasta.

name: The Criminology Museum
description: Ever hear of the Milazzo Cage?

name: Mirabilia Gallery
description: A wunderkammer of curious objects and intriguing art nestled within a hub of ancient Roman sites.

name: 'Mi Sono Perso' ('I Am Lost')
description: An enigmatic modern marker left next to a historic Roman site.

name: Burcardo Theater Museum
description: Harlequin costumes, signed scripts, and armor-wearing puppets.
